title: Sign-up Bonus vs Using the Correct Card for Every Purchase
author: Navees
summary: The whole 'use the right card for every purchase' thing is mostly a waste of time. Chase sign-up bonuses is where the real money is. Here's the math.
body_html:
Everyone obsesses over which card to tap for groceries vs dining vs gas. Honestly? It barely matters. The real money is in sign-up bonuses (SUBs), NOT what card you use day to day for each purchase.

There are only a few times it's actually worth pulling out a specific card:

  • You need travel insurance on the trip
  • You want rental car coverage
  • You want purchase protection
  • You want return protection
  • Paying rent through a platform that codes it well

Outside of that, stop stressing about categories. Let me show you why with numbers.

The math on a $24,000/year spend

Say you spend $24,000 a year and run it for two years.

Category-optimising approach: you get maybe ~$900/year in rewards value before fees. But once you stack up the annual fees on all those premium category cards, you're actually around -$430. Negative. You paid to play.

Sign-up bonus approach: across five cards you pull in about $4,215 in bonuses. After fees you net roughly $3,736.

Same spend. One strategy loses you money, the other nets you nearly four grand.

"But applying for cards is so much work." Is it though? It takes less effort than standing at a checkout doing mental math on which card earns 3x here. Apply, hit the spend, get the bonus, move on.

And those shiny premium perks — lounge access, shopping credits — do the math on whether they actually clear the annual fee for how you travel. For most people, they don't. I've written a whole separate post on this.
